Okay, I basically just... Without knowing, I started doing my thing. Okay, so that's that. So from my research, nothing that's exactly what the auto pipeline does regarding the architecture of the overall project with the event sourcing and the security and all of that.
But there are definitely interesting things that are worth mentioning and continuing to explore for both the building agents and for building the large-scale experiment on the weekend. So basically, there are parallel execution pipelines, some of which already have support for the air simulator, which is a keyskit, quantum circuit simulator, that will allow us to run the experiments in parallel, hopefully improving the performance of running, I don't know, millions of circuits.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: One thing to be aware of is the conversation I had with Jeff, and he mentioned not just the ability to run a large amount of information through a pipeline but the idea that it's a parallel execution within the pipeline.

content.speaker: Nicolas Berrogorry

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson

I don't think I know if we want to go over all of that right now.
I still have to go and take a look at every individual thing, but I will make the results available shortly. The summary is that there are pipelines that cover different areas. They cover experiment tracking, distributed computing, pipeline orchestration, and ancient orchestration. Nothing covers our pipeline orchestration quite the same.
Like a pipeline, but there are some things that theoretically we could make a stretch and use. But all of all, the pipeline is genetic. So it fits this perfectly. But the distributed computing, which is what you mentioned like Lash scale and parallel execution... I found a bunch of stuff, and some of that is natively compatible with KISS kit, which is what we're using.
So our backend may benefit extremely from that. The thing is called Dusk, and it's a Python package that you can leverage to do parallel execution.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Okay. I think the core goal here is to kind of get to two or three candidates that we want to take into a PC you have you seen.

content.speaker: Nicolas Berrogorry
content.text: Yeah, yes, yeah.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson

content.text:
I will close that one for tomorrow for sure. Then there is all the other thing that is completely separate. It's both separate from the auto pipeline and it's separate from the quantum parallel execution parallel parallelization, which is the ancient orchestration, which basically means we are trying to code ourselves, our own abstractions and all of that for the agents.
But when we are tasked with developing an actual production quality agent, we might be better off using either Landgraph or go ADK CRU, AI, Orogen, or Semantic Kernel, which are frameworks for developing agents.
Funny enough, every single thing that we are mentioning the auto pipeline, Dusk, Landgraph, or go, all of them handle the same problem in abstraction, which is input parallel execution as a sunset scatter gather and return and loop in the mirror sometimes, but they live at different layers of the architecture.
So the auto pipeline handles the input and output from the user, the coordination, and the parameterization of that. Then the Dusk handles the parallel execution of the secrets. If we want a sort of like an EA, an agent, like a MCP interface for the entire application, that's where we may want the two-layer SLA graph and all of those.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text:
Okay, let's hold. I'll hold thoughts on that for a second. I guess my question here is those two or three thinking around the work you did on the parallel circuit variant parallelization.
That's what we're bringing to the demo from your side.

content.speaker: Nicolas Berrogorry

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Okay?

content.speaker: Nicolas Berrogorry
